title: Flavio Mercati
text: Welcome to my personal site. I’m a [brief description]. Here I share research projects, side ideas, and notes.

title: Flat 3-manifolds, from the inside
text:
A companion to the Topological defects walkthrough, seen from the other side. Where that project floats tiny portal-glued defects in an open landscape and lets you fly through them, this one drops you inside a single fundamental cell of a closed flat 3-manifold. A King-Kai-style spherical planet sits at the centre of the cell; because the manifold is flat, the periodic images of that one planet seen receding through every wall are drawn literally, at each deck-group transform — no portal render pass, so the tiling is exact.
You are a small flying body, not a free-floating camera: a faint gravity pulls you toward the nearest planet, thrust and drag set a terminal velocity, and you coast and curve in when you let go. Fly toward a wall and you re-enter through its partner — translated, rotated, or mirror-reversed, depending on the cell.

title: Flat 3-manifolds, from the inside
text:
The mathematics
Each identified face carries a gluing isometry. We split it into a proper rotation and a reflection,
\[ g(\mathbf{x}) = D\,\big(R\,\mathbf{x}\big) + \mathbf{t}, \qquad
   D = \operatorname{diag}(\pm 1, \pm 1, \pm 1), \quad \det R = +1, \]
so that crossing a wall reorients the observer by the proper part \(R\) while the reflection part \(D\) flips handedness (parity) without ever being baked into the camera’s orientation. The crossing is instantaneous: the inside of a flat manifold is locally identical to ordinary space, so there is no jolt, spring or recovery wobble at the seam.
The cells are colour-coded by orientability — whether a consistent sense of handedness survives every loop:

yellow marks the orientable cells — the six flat platycosms, where every \(D = +\mathbf 1\) and only the rotation \(R\) acts;
green marks the non-orientable ones, where crossing a glide-reflected wall (\(\det D = -1\)) hands the world back mirror-reversed, so “left” and “right” stop being globally defined. The whole scene is then rendered through a reflection about the vertical plane through the camera; cross such a wall twice and the reversal cancels.

title: Flat 3-manifolds, from the inside
text:
Explore




Gallery → — a grid of stills, one per manifold. Read each plaque, then click to drop inside that cell. (Works on a phone.)
Open the simulation → — take the observer’s seat in the 3-torus straight away. (Desktop; mouse + keyboard. Append ?topo=half, ?topo=amphi1, etc. to start inside another cell.)

title: Flat 3-manifolds, from the inside
text:
The cells
Nine fundamental domains, all of them closed, flat, and prime — the space forms you get by gluing a Euclidean solid to itself.

Orientable (the six platycosms) — the 3-torus \(T^3\), the half-, third-, quarter- and sixth-turn flat space forms, and the Hantzsche–Wendt didicosm that completes the list (a rhombic-dodecahedral cell glued by three perpendicular half-turn screws — the one cell here with no central seam).
Non-orientable — three of the four non-orientable flat manifolds: the first amphicosm (Klein bottle \(\times S^1\)), the second amphicosm (its swap-glide sibling, glued across the diagonal mirror \(x=y\)), and the first amphidicosm, the mirror counterpart of the Hantzsche–Wendt cell. Cross a glide-reflected wall in any of them and the world comes back left–right reversed.

The twisted cube cells (quarter-, half-, sixth- and third-turn) are shown as flattened stand-ins: all their face-pairs twist, so their edges carry conical seams (deficit angles) the smooth manifold has not. That is the honest physics of such a cell, not a rendering artefact — the same compromise the defects walkthrough documents.

title: Topological defects: flying through space glued to itself
text:
A topological defect is a region of space that has been cut out and re-glued to itself. Its boundary faces are identified in pairs by rigid motions, so light — and an observer — that leaves through one wall returns through the partner wall, possibly rotated. The simplest case is a cube whose opposite faces are glued straight across by translation: a perfectly transparent block that simply displaces whatever you see through it. Twist the gluing, or glue the faces of a different polyhedron, and you get a whole zoo of richer defects — the space forms and geons that classify the closed 3-manifolds.
This project makes those abstract identifications something you can see and move through. You take the seat of a free-floating camera in a stylized open world — rolling hills, a winding river under a stone bridge, cottages, a ring of mountains, drifting clouds — and nine defects float above the meadow, each marked by a signpost. Fly through one and the full gluing is applied to you, orientation included; the view then rights itself with a short, springy wobble.

title: Topological defects: flying through space glued to itself
text:
The mathematics
Each identified face carries a gluing isometry — a rotation \(R\) and a translation \(\mathbf{t}\), applied in the defect’s local frame as
\[ g(\mathbf{x}) = R\,\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{t}. \]
The familiar “twist about the face’s own normal” family is the special case \(R = R(\hat{\mathbf n},\theta)\), \(\mathbf{t} = -D\,\hat{\mathbf n}\); the more exotic cells supply an arbitrary \((R,\mathbf{t})\) — and for the non-orientable cells \(R\) is a reflection (\(\det R = -1\)), which is exactly what hands the world back mirror-reversed as you cross.
The signs are colour-coded by orientability — whether a consistent sense of handedness survives every loop through the cell:

yellow signs mark the orientable cells — here, all six flat platycosms;
green signs mark the non-orientable ones, where crossing a glide-reflected wall hands the world back mirror-reversed, so “left” and “right” stop being globally defined.

Written into each yellow sign is a second property, spinoriality — whether the cell’s underlying 3-manifold admits half-integer spin, the Friedman–Sorkin mechanism by which topology alone lets a region of space behave like a fermion. The classification is clean: by Hendriks’ theorem the only non-spinorial prime 3-manifolds are \(S^3\), \(S^2\times S^1\), and the lens spaces, so every flat orientable cell here is spinorial and its sign reads (spin \(\tfrac12\)); the (spin 1) tag returns with the lens spaces. The non-orientable cells carry pin± rather than spin structure, so they take no tag.

title: Topological defects: flying through space glued to itself
text:
Explore




Navigator → — the full open world. Take the observer’s seat and fly through all nine defects in one continuous landscape. (Desktop; mouse + keyboard.)
Gallery → — a grid of windows, one per manifold. Orbit each defect on its own and read its plaque. (Works beautifully on a phone.)
Documentation → — the mathematics of the defects, the story of how the visualization was built (wormholes and all), and the rendering technology behind it.

title: Topological defects: flying through space glued to itself
text:
The defects
Nine gluing-cells, all of them closed, flat, and prime — the space forms you get by gluing a Euclidean solid to itself.

Orientable (the six platycosms) — the 3-torus \(T^3\), the half-, third-, quarter- and sixth-turn flat space forms, and the Hantzsche–Wendt didicosm that completes the list.
Non-orientable — three of the four non-orientable flat manifolds: the first amphicosm (Klein bottle \(\times S^1\)), the second amphicosm (its swap-glide sibling, glued across a diagonal mirror), and the first amphidicosm, the mirror counterpart of the Hantzsche–Wendt cell. Cross a glide-reflected wall in any of them and the world comes back left–right reversed; cross it twice and the reversal cancels.

The twisted cube cells (the quarter- and half-turn) are shown as flattened stand-ins — all three face-pairs twist, so their edges carry conical seams (deficit angles) the smooth manifold has not. That is the real physics of such a defect, not a rendering artefact; the documentation explains where the honesty line falls, why the curved space forms were pulled out pending a faithful rebuild, and why four of the eight Thurston geometries can’t be shown this way at all.

title: About
text: I’m a researcher at the University of Burgos, Spain working on quantum gravity.

title: Tessera: an alphabet of 3×3 binary grids
text: Tessera is a constructed writing system whose characters are the 3×3 binary pixel grids, classified under the symmetries of the square.

title: Tessera: an alphabet of 3×3 binary grids
text:
The mathematics
A 3×3 binary grid has \(2^9 = 512\) possible states. Two grids are considered the same character if one can be obtained from the other by a rotation or reflection — that is, by an element of the dihedral group \(D_4\). The number of distinct characters is the number of orbits of \(D_4\) acting on the 512 grids, which Burnside’s lemma counts as
\[
\frac{1}{|D_4|}\sum_{g \in D_4} |\mathrm{Fix}(g)|
= \frac{1}{8}\left(2^9 + 2\cdot 2^3 + 2^5 + 2\cdot 2^6 + 2\cdot 2^5\right)
= \frac{816}{8} = 102.
\]
Those 102 orbits are the characters of Tessera. Excluding the empty grid and adding case and orientation variants, the full system maps onto 128 characters covering the Latin alphabet, digits, punctuation, IPA symbols and accented letters. Every orbit has a name — from CARDINAL and ROCKET to BELLFLOWER, whose orbit contains the canonical glider from Conway’s Game of Life.
One pleasant invariant: the center pixel is the unique fixed point of \(D_4\) acting on the grid cells, so “is the center filled?” is constant on every orbit — it cleanly separates the alphabet into two families.

title: Tessera: an alphabet of 3×3 binary grids
text:
Try it
The font is real: TrueType outlines generated programmatically from the same JSON source of truth that produces all the documents below.
✒️ Type in Tessera — interactive specimen — a live demo with the full family (Regular, Bold, Italic, Bold Italic) embedded. Type anything and watch it render.

title: Tessera: an alphabet of 3×3 binary grids
text:
Documents

Periodic table of characters — all 102 orbits arranged by structure
Orbit explorer — every orbit with its full set of grid representatives
Calligraphic forms — the stroke-based rendering algorithm
Notes on the Tessera — the full mathematical write-up
